---
title: "Getting Started with remote sensing"
format: html
---

## Weekly Learning Diary

::: callout-note
## Summary

This week provided an introduction to remote sensing as a concept, focusing on how the Earth can be observed without direct contact. The lecture explored how sensors can be mounted on different platforms, including satellites, aircraft, and drones, and introduced the distinction between passive and active sensing. Passive sensors rely on naturally occurring radiation such as sunlight, while active sensors emit their own signal; however, both approaches have limitations, including dependency on environmental conditions for passive systems and increased complexity and cost for active systems.

A key focus was on electromagnetic radiation and how it interacts with the Earth’s surface, producing distinct spectral signatures that allow different materials to be identified. This was supported by discussion of atmospheric processes such as scattering, as well as practical challenges including cloud cover and varying spatial, temporal, and spectral resolutions. Together, these concepts highlighted both the potential of remote sensing for large-scale observation and the constraints that affect data quality and interpretation.

The practical aimed to apply these principles through working with Landsat and Sentinel imagery. This included sourcing and comparing datasets, undertaking basic raster processing and statistics, evaluating the advantages and disadvantages of different software, and analysing spectral signatures. Initially, getting to grips with Copernicus was challenging due to the number of options and the process of identifying and downloading suitable data, which was also slow. I then found SNAP difficult to use, particularly when loading and processing the data, which limited my ability to fully complete the workflow. However, using R to explore spectral signatures helped reinforce how remotely sensed data can be analysed quantitatively rather than purely visually.

However, several limitations were evident. Remote sensing is highly dependent on data availability and quality, with cloud cover, atmospheric effects, and resolution all impacting reliability. In addition, the complexity of accessing and processing data, as well as the usability of tools such as SNAP, can present barriers to effective use. These challenges highlight the importance of both data considerations and technical capability when applying remote sensing in real-world contexts.
:::

::: callout-tip
## Applications

Remote sensing plays a key role in environmental and urban monitoring by allowing large areas to be observed consistently over time. One of its most common applications is land use and land cover classification, where spectral signatures are used to distinguish between materials such as vegetation, water, and built-up areas. For example, satellite imagery from platforms such as Landsat has been widely used to map urban expansion and monitor land use change over time, as demonstrated in long-term satellite analyses (e.g. [Wulder et al. (2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0034425719300707)[)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0034425718302352)). This ability to consistently observe change supports decision-making in areas such as urban planning and environmental management.

These techniques are also applied in environmental monitoring, where differences in spectral response can indicate changes in vegetation health or water quality. Vegetation indices derived from satellite imagery, such as NDVI, have been widely used to assess ecosystem condition and detect environmental change (e.g. [Pettorelli et al. (2005)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S016953470500162X)). In practice, similar principles are also applied using other remote sensing data sources such as LiDAR. For example, in a planning enforcement context, I have used LiDAR data to measure changes in surface height in order to provide evidence of unauthorised development. This highlights how remotely sensed data can be applied in regulatory and decision-making processes. However, the effectiveness of these applications is influenced by factors such as atmospheric interference, cloud cover, and the spatial and temporal resolution of the data. This highlights the importance of selecting appropriate data sources and understanding their limitations when applying remote sensing in real-world contexts.
:::

::: callout-important
## Reflection

This week helped build a clearer understanding of how remote sensing moves from theory into practice, particularly how different data sources and tools influence what can actually be achieved. While the concepts themselves were relatively straightforward, I found the practical more challenging, especially when working with SNAP. Difficulties loading and processing the data made it harder to follow the full workflow, which highlighted how important usability and reliability are when working with real-world datasets. In contrast, I found working in R more intuitive, reinforcing my preference for coding-based approaches where processes are more transparent and reproducible.

What stood out most was how remote sensing enables large-scale and repeatable analysis that would not be feasible through traditional data collection methods. In a local government context, this has clear value. Access to satellite data through sources such as the Earth Observation Data Hub (EODH), alongside other remotely sensed datasets available under the Public Sector Geospatial Agreement (PSGA), creates opportunities to monitor environmental change, urban development, and land use more consistently across the borough. This could support areas such as planning, enforcement, and environmental monitoring by providing an additional layer of evidence to complement existing GIS datasets.

However, this week also made it clear that access to data alone is not enough. The challenges experienced during the practical demonstrated that the choice of tools and the ability to process and interpret the data are equally important. Moving forward, I would be more inclined to explore platforms such as Google Earth Engine or integrate remote sensing into existing GIS and database workflows, where analysis can be scaled and automated more effectively. Overall, this week highlighted both the potential and the practical limitations of applying remote sensing in real-world settings.
:::
